examples.title: Merge Sort Algorithm
examples.description: Divide and Conquer sorting algorithm with O(n log n) complexity
examples.code:
MERGE-SORT(A, p, r)
BEGIN
  IF p < r THEN
    q = (p + r) / 2
    MERGE-SORT(A, p, q)
    MERGE-SORT(A, q+1, r)
    MERGE(A, p, q, r)
  ENDIF
END

MERGE(A, p, q, r)
BEGIN
  n1 = q - p + 1
  n2 = r - q
  Create arrays L[1..n1+1] and R[1..n2+1]
  for i = 1 to n1: L[i] = A[p + i - 1]
  for j = 1 to n2: R[j] = A[q + j]
  L[n1+1] = ∞, R[n2+1] = ∞
  i = 1, j = 1
  for k = p to r
    if L[i] <= R[j] then
      A[k] = L[i]; i = i + 1
    else
      A[k] = R[j]; j = j + 1
    endif
  endfor
END
examples.explanation: MERGE-SORT recursively divides array until single elements, then MERGE combines sorted halves. The divide is internal (done in same function) and merge is external (done after recursive calls).

examples.title: Binary Search Implementation
examples.description: Classic divide and conquer search in sorted array
examples.explanation: Each recursive call divides the search space in half. The combine step is trivial as we simply return the result from the appropriate subproblem.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(log n) for recursion stack, O(1) for iterative version

examples.title: Quick Sort Algorithm
examples.description: In-place divide and conquer sorting with partition-based division
examples.code:
QUICK-SORT(A, p, r)
BEGIN
  IF p < r THEN
    q = PARTITION(A, p, r)
    QUICK-SORT(A, p, q - 1)
    QUICK-SORT(A, q + 1, r)
  ENDIF
END

PARTITION(A, p, r)
BEGIN
  x = A[r]  // pivot element
  i = p - 1
  for j = p to r - 1
    if A[j] <= x then
      i = i + 1
      swap A[i] with A[j]
    endif
  endfor
  swap A[i + 1] with A[r]
  return i + 1
END

RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
BEGIN
  i = RANDOM(p, r)
  swap A[r] with A[i]
  return PARTITION(A, p, r)
END
examples.explanation: PARTITION divides array based on pivot. Elements less than pivot go left, greater go right. Quick Sort then recursively sorts both parts. Randomized version swaps random element to end before partitioning.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst

examples.title: Closest Pair of Points - Divide and Conquer
examples.description: Find closest pair of points in O(n log n) time using divide and conquer
examples.code:
CLOSEST-POINT(Px, Py)
// Px sorted by x, Py sorted by y
BEGIN
  IF |P| <= 3 THEN
    return BRUTE-FORCE(P)  // Base case
  ENDIF
  
  // DIVIDE: split by x-coordinate
  mid = |Px| / 2
  Qx = first mid points of Px
  Rx = remaining points of Px
  
  // CONQUER: recursively find closest in each half
  dl = CLOSEST-POINT(Qx, filter_by_y(Qx, Py))
  dr = CLOSEST-POINT(Rx, filter_by_y(Rx, Py))
  d = min(dl, dr)
  
  // COMBINE: build strip and find closest across divide
  P_star = points within distance d of dividing line
  sort P_star by y-coordinate
  for i = 1 to |P_star| - 1
    for j = 1 to min(7, |P_star| - i)
      if distance(P_star[i], P_star[i+j]) < d
        d = distance(P_star[i], P_star[i+j])
      endif
    endfor
  endfor
  
  return d
END
examples.explanation: Points are sorted by x-coordinate (Px) and y-coordinate (Py). After recursive calls find closest pairs in halves, the combine step checks points within distance d of the dividing line. Only 7 subsequent points need checking in the strip.

examples.title: Median of Medians Algorithm
examples.description: Find kth element in O(n) worst-case using divide and conquer
examples.explanation: Divides array into groups of 5, finds medians of each group, then recursively finds median of these medians. This guarantees pivot splits work into 30%-70% or better, giving O(n) worst-case. Used for guaranteed O(n log n) Quick Sort.
